hangulBasic.title: Hangul Basics
hangulBasic.content:
### **The Hangul System** 📝

Hangul is a logical phonetic alphabet. Characters are grouped into blocks, and each block must contain at least one consonant and one vowel.

#### **Basic & Derived Vowels**

Vowels are based on three elements: Man (ㅣ), Earth (ㅡ), and Heaven (ㆍ).

- `<speak='ㅏ'>` *(a)*
- `<speak='ㅓ'>` *(eo - as in 'up')*
- `<speak='ㅗ'>` *(o - as in 'home')*
- `<speak='ㅜ'>` *(u - as in 'moon')*
- `<speak='ㅣ'>` *(i - as in 'meet')*
- `<speak='ㅡ'>` *(eu - guttural sound)*
- `<speak='ㅔ'>` *(e - as in 'bed')*
- `<speak='ㅐ'>` *(ae - as in 'apple')*

#### **Basic Consonants**

| Letter | Sound / Audio | Name |
|--------|---------------|------|
| `<speak='ㄱ'>` | G/K → `<speak='그'>` | Giyeok |
| `<speak='ㄴ'>` | N → `<speak='느'>` | Nieun |
| `<speak='ㄷ'>` | D/T → `<speak='드'>` | Digueut |
| `<speak='ㄹ'>` | R/L → `<speak='르'>` | Rieul |
| `<speak='ㅁ'>` | M → `<speak='므'>` | Mieum |
| `<speak='ㅂ'>` | B/P → `<speak='브'>` | Bieup |
| `<speak='ㅅ'>` | S → `<speak='스'>` | Siot |
| `<speak='ㅇ'>` | -/NG → `<speak='응'>` | Ieung |
| `<speak='ㅈ'>` | J → `<speak='즈'>` | Jieut |
| `<speak='ㅎ'>` | H → `<speak='흐'>` | Hieut |

#### **Double Vowels (Diphthongs)**

Combined sounds that require precision:

- `<speak='ㅒ'>` *(yae)*
- `<speak='ㅖ'>` *(ye)*
- `<speak='ㅘ'>` *(wa)*
- `<speak='ㅝ'>` *(wo)*
- `<speak='ㅙ'>` *(wae)*
- `<speak='ㅞ'>` *(we)*
- `<speak='ㅟ'>` *(wi)*
- `<speak='ㅢ'>` *(ui)*

#### **Aspirated & Double Consonants**

**Aspirated** consonants have a burst of air. **Double (Ssang)** consonants are tense and airless.

| Type | Letters | Sound / Audio |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Aspirated** | `<speak='ㅋ'>`, `<speak='ㅌ'>`, `<speak='ㅍ'>`, `<speak='ㅊ'>` | `<speak='크'>`, `<speak='트'>`, `<speak='프'>`, `<speak='츠'>` |
| **Double** | `<speak='ㄲ'>`, `<speak='ㄸ'>`, `<speak='ㅃ'>`, `<speak='ㅆ'>`, `<speak='ㅉ'>` | `<speak='끄'>`, `<speak='뜨'>`, `<speak='쁘'>`, `<speak='쓰'>`, `<speak='쯔'>` |

#### **Practical Examples**

- `<speak='어머니'>` *(Mother)*
- `<speak='아기'>` *(Baby)*
- `<speak='우유'>` *(Milk)*
- `<speak='의자'>` *(Chair)*
- `<speak='커피'>` *(Coffee)*
- `<speak='아빠'>` *(Dad)*

> **Rule of Thumb:** Aspirated consonants usually have an extra stroke compared to their basic form. Double consonants are just the basic form mirrored!

hanja.title: Hanja
hanja.content:
### **Historical and Cultural Influence** 📜

For centuries, Korea was deeply influenced by China's cultural sphere. Until the last century, Koreans primarily used Chinese characters, known as **Hanja** (한자), for formal writing.

**Past:** `<speak='大韓民國'>` 
**Present:** `<speak='대한민국'>` 

### **Why is Hanja still useful?** 💡

Hanja provides the building blocks for over **60% of the Korean vocabulary**. It is similar to how English uses Latin or Greek roots to form complex words.

Consider a made-up word like **"Bio-Neo-Tech-Dog"**. Each syllable adds a specific layer of meaning:
- **Bio**: Life
- **Neo**: New
- **Tech**: Technology
- **Dog**: Animal

Hanja functions exactly like this, offering high informational density in a single syllable.

---

### **Case Study: 대한민국** 🇰🇷

The name for South Korea (Republic of Korea) is a combination of four meanings:
- **大** (`<speak='대'>`): Big/Great
- **韓** (`<speak='한'>`): Korean People
- **民** (`<speak='민'>`): People/Citizens
- **國** (`<speak='국'>`): Nation/Country

> **민** (People) + **국** (Country) = **민국** (Republic/Democracy).

---

### **Most Common Hanja** ✍️

| Character | Hangul | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|---|
| `<speak='大'>` | **대** | Big |
| `<speak='中'>` | **중** | Medium |
| `<speak='小'>` | **소** | Small |
| `<speak='月'>` | **월** | Moon (Monday) |
| `<speak='火'>` | **화** | Fire (Tuesday) |
| `<speak='水'>` | **수** | Water (Wednesday) |
| `<speak='木'>` | **목** | Wood (Thursday) |
| `<speak='金'>` | **금** | Gold/Metal (Friday) |
| `<speak='土'>` | **토** | Earth (Saturday) |
| `<speak='日'>` | **일** | Sun/Day (Sunday) |
| `<speak='上'>` | **상** | Up/Top |
| `<speak='下'>` | **하** | Down/Bottom |
| `<speak='左'>` | **좌** | Left |
| `<speak='右'>` | **우** | Right |
| `<speak='前'>` | **전** | Front/Former |
| `<speak='後'>` | **후** | Behind/After |
| `<speak='市'>` | **시** | City |
| `<speak='人'>` | **인** | Person |
| `<speak='山'>` | **산** | Mountain |
| `<speak='門'>` | **문** | Gate |
| `<speak='本'>` | **본** | Root/Book |

batchim.title: Batchim
batchim.content:
### **What is Batchim?** 📝

In Hangul, **Batchim** (받침) refers to the consonants at the bottom of a block. They are simplified into **7 main sounds**.

#### **The 7 Sounds of Batchim**

| Sound | Represented Letters | Sound Sample |
|:---:|---------------------|--------------|
| **[k]** | `<speak='ㄱ'>`, `<speak='ㅋ'>`, `<speak='ㄲ'>` | `<speak='억'>` |
| **[n]** | `<speak='ㄴ'>` | `<speak='언'>` |
| **[t]** | `<speak='ㄷ'>`, `<speak='ㅅ'>`, `<speak='ㅆ'>`, `<speak='ㅈ'>`, `<speak='ㅊ'>`, `<speak='ㅌ'>`, `<speak='ㅎ'>` | `<speak='얻'>` |
| **[l]** | `<speak='ㄹ'>` | `<speak='얼'>` |
| **[m]** | `<speak='ㅁ'>` | `<speak='엄'>` |
| **[p]** | `<speak='ㅂ'>`, `<speak='ㅍ'>` | `<speak='업'>` |
| **[ng]**| `<speak='ㅇ'>` | `<speak='엉'>` |

---

### **The Special Case of ㅎ (Aspiration)** 💨

When `<speak='ㅎ'>` is in the Batchim position (either alone or in doubles like **ㄴㅎ**, **ㄹㅎ**) and meets certain consonants in the next syllable, they combine to create an **aspirated** sound (a burst of air).

- **ㅎ + ㄱ = ㅋ**
- **ㅎ + ㄷ = ㅌ**
- **ㅎ + ㅈ = ㅊ**
- **ㅎ + ㅂ = ㅍ**

**Key Examples:**
- `<speak='축하해'>`: Written Chuk-ha-hae | Pronounced: **Chu-ka-hae** (Congratulations)
- `<speak='많다'>`: Written Man-da | Pronounced: **Man-ta** (To be many)
- `<speak='좋다'>`: Written Joh-da | Pronounced: **Jo-ta** (To be good)
- `<speak='입학'>`: Written Ip-hak | Pronounced: **I-pak** (School admission)

---

### **Double Batchim (겹받침)** ⚠️

There are 11 combinations. Usually, only **one** is pronounced unless followed by a vowel.

#### **1. Pronouncing the FIRST consonant**
- **ㄴㅈ**: `<speak='앉다'>` (An-da) - *To sit*
- **ㄹㅂ**: `<speak='여덟'>` (Yeo-deol) - *Eight*
- **ㅂㅅ**: `<speak='값'>` (Gap) - *Price*
- **ㄱㅅ**: `<speak='몫'>` (Mok) - *Share*

#### **2. Pronouncing the SECOND consonant**
- **ㄹㄱ**: `<speak='닭'>` (Dak) - *Chicken*
- **ㄹㅁ**: `<speak='삶'>` (Sam) - *Life*
- **ㄹㅍ**: `<speak='읊다'>` (Up-da) - *To recite*

colors.title: Colors
colors.content:
### **Lesson 2: Colors (색깔)** 🎨

In Korean, the word for color is **색 (saek)** or **색깔 (saek-kkal)**. Many times, we add "saek" to the name of the color to identify it clearly.

#### 1. Main Colors List

| Color | Korean | Pronunciation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Red | 빨간색 | `<speak='빨간색'>` |
| Blue | 파란색 | `<speak='파란색'>` |
| Yellow | 노란색 | `<speak='노란색'>` |
| Green | 초록색 | `<speak='초록색'>` |
| Black | 검정색 | `<speak='검정색'>` |
| White | 하얀색 | `<speak='하얀색'>` |

#### 2. Grammatical Curiosity
Colors in Korean can act as nouns or adjectives. 
* **Noun:** "I like blue" (파란색을 좋아해요).
* **Adjective:** When the color ends in **ㄴ** (like 빨간), it's already ready to describe something immediately: **빨간 사과** (Red apple).

numbers.title: Native Numbers
numbers.content:
### **Native Korean Numbers (1-99)** 🔢

Used for counting physical items, people, and age. 

#### **1 to 10**
| Number | Korean | Number | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| **1** | `<speak='하나'>` | **6** | `<speak='여섯'>` |
| **2** | `<speak='둘'>` | **7** | `<speak='일곱'>` |
| **3** | `<speak='셋'>` | **8** | `<speak='여덟'>` |
| **4** | `<speak='넷'>` | **9** | `<speak='아홉'>` |
| **5** | `<speak='다섯'>` | **10** | `<speak='열'>` |

#### **The Tens**
| Value | Korean | Value | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| **20** | `<speak='스물'>` | **60** | `<speak='예순'>` |
| **30** | `<speak='서른'>` | **70** | `<speak='일흔'>` |
| **40** | `<speak='마흔'>` | **80** | `<speak='여든'>` |
| **50** | `<speak='쉰'>` | **90** | `<speak='아흔'>` |

---

### **Ordinals and Short Forms** 🥇

To create ordinals, we add **`<speak='번째'>`**. Some numbers shorten their form when used as ordinals or before counters:

- 1: `<speak='하나'>` → **`<speak='한'>`** (Ex: `<speak='첫 번째'>` - 1st)
- 2: `<speak='둘'>` → **`<speak='두'>`** (Ex: `<speak='두 번째'>` - 2nd)
- 3: `<speak='셋'>` → **`<speak='세'>`** (Ex: `<speak='세 번째'>` - 3rd)
- 4: `<speak='넷'>` → **`<speak='네'>`** (Ex: `<speak='네 번째'>` - 4th)
- 20: `<speak='스물'>` → **`<speak='스무'>`** (Ex: `<speak='스무 번째'>` - 20th)

---

### **Essential Counters** 📦

Structure: **Noun + Number (short form) + Counter**.

| Counter | Description | Example |
|:---:|-----------|---------|
| `<speak='개'>` | General objects | `<speak='사과 세 개'>` (3 apples) |
| `<speak='명'>` | People | `<speak='학생 세 명'>` (3 students) |
| `<speak='마리'>` | Animals | `<speak='고양이 세 마리'>` (3 cats) |
| `<speak='권'>` | Books | `<speak='책 세 권'>` (3 books) |
| `<speak='대'>` | Machines/Cars | `<speak='세탁기 세 대'>` (3 machines) |
| `<speak='장'>` | Flat objects | `<speak='도화지 세 장'>` (3 sheets) |
| `<speak='병'>` | Bottles | `<speak='맥주 세 병'>` (3 bottles) |
| `<speak='잔'>` | Cups/Glasses | `<speak='커피 세 잔'>` (3 cups) |
| `<speak='벌'>` | Clothing items | `<speak='잠옷 세 벌'>` (3 pajamas) |
| `<speak='줄'>` | Things in lines | `<speak='김밥 세 줄'>` (3 kimbaps) |

sinoNumbers.title: Sino Numbers
sinoNumbers.content:
### **Sino-Korean System (Hanja)** 💰

Unlike native numbers, Sino-Korean numbers are used for **money, dates, minutes, addresses, and any number above 100**.

#### **Base Units**
| Number | Korean | Number | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| **1** | `<speak='일'>` | **6** | `<speak='육'>` |
| **2** | `<speak='이'>` | **7** | `<speak='칠'>` |
| **3** | `<speak='삼'>` | **8** | `<speak='팔'>` |
| **4** | `<speak='사'>` | **9** | `<speak='구'>` |
| **5** | `<speak='오'>` | **10** | `<speak='십'>` |

---

### **The 4-Zero Rule: 만 (Man)** 📈

In English, we group numbers by 3 zeros (thousands). In Korean, the main unit change happens every **four zeros**. This is the key to mastering large numbers and money.

| Value | Korean | Equivalent |
|:---:|---|---|
| **100** | `<speak='백'>` | Hundred |
| **1,000** | `<speak='천'>` | Thousand |
| **10,000** | `<speak='만'>` | Ten Thousand (Base unit) |
| **100,000,000** | `<speak='억'>` | Hundred Million |

> **Visualization:** 
> Instead of 10,000, think of it as **1,0000**. 
> 50,000 Won is not "fifty thousand," but **5 ten-thousands**: `<speak='오만 원'>`.

---

### **Practicing with Money** 💳

The currency of Korea is **Won** (`<speak='원'>`). Notice how the logic scales:

- **150** (100 + 50): `<speak='백오십'>` (Baek-o-sip)
- **1,500** (1000 + 500): `<speak='천오백'>` (Cheon-o-baek)
- **15,000** (1,5000): `<speak='만 오천'>` (Man-o-cheon)
- **1,200,000** (120,0000): `<speak='백이십만'>` (Baek-i-sip-man)

### **Pro Tip** 💡
When reading large numbers in Korean, count 4 digits from the right. That's where **만** (Man) starts!

bodyParts.title: Body Parts
bodyParts.content:
### **Head and Face** 👤

Starting from the top. Here is the most common vocabulary for the face:

| Part | Korean | Part | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| Head | `<speak='머리'>` | Eye | `<speak='눈'>` |
| Nose | `<speak='코'>` | Mouth | `<speak='입'>` |
| Ear | `<speak='귀'>` | Face | `<speak='얼굴'>` |

---

### **Body and Limbs** 🦒

| Part | Korean | Part | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| Neck | `<speak='목'>` | Shoulder | `<speak='어깨'>` |
| Arm | `<speak='팔'>` | Hand | `<speak='손'>` |
| Leg | `<speak='다리'>` | Foot | `<speak='발'>` |
| Knee | `<speak='무릎'>` | Waist | `<speak='허리'>` |

---

### **The Logic of -가락 (Garak)** 🖐️

The suffix **가락** implies something long and thin (like a strand or a stick). It’s used to name fingers and toes:

- **Hand** (`<speak='손'>`) + **가락** = **`<speak='손가락'>`** (Finger)
- **Foot** (`<speak='발'>`) + **가락** = **`<speak='발가락'>`** (Toe)
- **Hair**: We use **`<speak='머리카락'>`** (Literally: head strands).

---

### **Quick Tip: Expressing Pain** 🤒

Simply combine the body part with **아파요** (apayo) to say it hurts:

- `<speak='머리 아파요'>` (Headache)
- `<speak='목 아파요'>` (Sore throat / Neck pain)
- `<speak='배 아파요'>` (Stomachache - **`<speak='배'>`** means stomach!)

animals.title: Animals
animals.content:
### **Common Animals** 🐾

| Animal | Korean | Animal | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| Dog | `<speak='개'>` | Cat | `<speak='고양이'>` |
| Bird | `<speak='새'>` | Rabbit | `<speak='토끼'>` |
| Tiger | `<speak='호랑이'>` | Lion | `<speak='사자'>` |
| Bear | `<speak='곰'>` | Elephant | `<speak='코끼리'>` |
| Pig | `<speak='돼지'>` | Cow | `<speak='소'>` |

---

### **Naming Baby Animals** 🐣

There are two main ways to name babies in Korean:

#### **1. General Rule: 새끼 (Saekki)**
For most animals, add **새끼** after the name:
- `<speak='새끼 고양이'>` (Kitten)
- `<speak='새끼 곰'>` (Bear cub)
- `<speak='새끼 호랑이'>` (Tiger cub)

#### **2. Historical Exceptions (Suffix -아지)**
Animals historically close to Korean life have unique names for their young:
- **Dog** (`<speak='개'>`) → **`<speak='강아지'>`** (Puppy)
- **Cow** (`<speak='소'>`) → **`<speak='송아지'>`** (Calf)
- **Horse** (`<speak='말'>`) → **`<speak='망아지'>`** (Foal)

> **⚠️ DANGER:** Never apply the general rule to dogs. The word **개새끼** is a very strong curse word. Always use **강아지**!

---

### **Male vs. Female Animals** ♂️♀️

1. **수- (Su)**: Male (e.g., `<speak='수사자'>` - Male lion)
2. **암- (Am)**: Female (e.g., `<speak='암사자'>` - Lioness)

---

### **Animal Sounds** 🔊

- 🐶 `<speak='멍멍'>` (Dog)
- 🐱 `<speak='야옹'>` (Cat)
- 🐷 `<speak='꿀꿀'>` (Pig)
- 🐥 `<speak='삐약삐약'>` (Chick)

family.title: Family
family.content:
### **Immediate Family** 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦

Core base terms. Note: Koreans often use **우리** (our) instead of "my" when talking about their family (e.g., "Our mother").

| Member | Korean | Member | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| Parents | `<speak='부모님'>` | Grandfather | `<speak='할아버지'>` |
| Father | `<speak='아버지'>` | Grandmother | `<speak='할머니'>` |
| Mother | `<speak='어머니'>` | Younger Sibling | `<speak='동생'>` |

---

### **Siblings: It depends on YOU!** 👫

The terms for older siblings change based on the speaker's gender.

#### **If you are MALE:**
- Older Brother: **`<speak='형'>`** (Hyeong)
- Older Sister: **`<speak='누나'>`** (Nuna)

#### **If you are FEMALE:**
- Older Brother: **`<speak='오빠'>`** (Oppa)
- Older Sister: **`<speak='언니'>`** (Eonni)

---

### **Cultural Context: Hierarchy and Honorifics** 🙇‍♂️

1. **Age Matters:** In Korea, you rarely call someone older by their name. Even with friends, you use family terms (Oppa, Eonni, etc.) to show respect for their age.
2. **The -님 (Nim) Suffix:** To show extra respect to someone else's parents, add **님** (e.g., `<speak='아버님'>`, `<speak='어머님'>`).

---

### **Pro Tips** 💡

- **Everyday Use:** These terms aren't just for blood relatives. Close friends use them to strengthen their bond.
- **Casual Terms:** For "Dad" and "Mom," use **`<speak='아빠'>`** (Appa) and **`<speak='엄마'>`** (Eomma).
- **Spouses:** A common term of endearment between husband and wife is **`<speak='여보'>`** (Yeobo - Honey/Darling).

foodDrinks.title: Food and Drink
foodDrinks.content:
### **Essential Vocabulary** 🍚

| Item | Korean | Item | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| Rice (cooked) | `<speak='밥'>` | Water | `<speak='물'>` |
| Soup | `<speak='국'>` | Tea | `<speak='차'>` |
| Meat | `<speak='고기'>` | Vegetables | `<speak='야채'>` |
| Bread | `<speak='빵'>` | Milk | `<speak='우유'>` |

---

### **The World of Konglish** 🍔

Modern Korean food culture is full of English loanwords adapted to Korean phonology:

- `<speak='커피'>` (Keo-pi) = **Coffee**
- `<speak='주스'>` (Ju-seu) = **Juice**
- `<speak='콜라'>` (Kol-la) = **Cola**
- `<speak='햄버거'>` (Haem-beo-geo) = **Hamburger**
- `<speak='피자'>` (Pi-ja) = **Pizza**
- `<speak='아이스크림'>` (A-i-seu-keu-rim) = **Ice cream**

---

### **Word Construction Logic** 💡

Notice how Korean builds specific meat types by combining the animal name with **고기** (Meat):

- **Cow** (`<speak='소'>`) + **Meat** = **`<speak='소고기'>`** (Beef)
- **Pig** (`<speak='돼지'>`) + **Meat** = **`<speak='돼지고기'>`** (Pork)
- **Chicken** (`<speak='닭'>`) + **Meat** = **`<speak='닭고기'>`** (Chicken meat)

---

### **Pro Tips** 🥢

1. **Dining Etiquette:** Before eating, always say **`<speak='잘 먹겠습니다'>`** (I will eat well) to show appreciation to the host.
2. **Mat-jip (맛집):** Literally "Taste House." If you see people lining up at a restaurant, it's likely a famous **Mat-jip**!
3. **Chimaek (치맥):** The ultimate food pairing in Korea. It combines **Chi**cken + **Maek**ju (Beer). Try it if you haven't!

clothing.title: Clothing
clothing.content:
### **Basic Vocabulary: 옷 (Ot)** 👕

The general word for clothes is **옷**. Here are the essentials:

| Item | Korean | Item | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| Pants | `<speak='바지'>` | Skirt | `<speak='치마'>` |
| Shirt | `<speak='셔츠'>` | Socks | `<speak='양말'>` |
| Shoes | `<speak='신발'>` | Hat | `<speak='모자'>` |
| Underwear | `<speak='속옷'>` | Pajamas | `<speak='잠옷'>` |

---

### **Seasonal Wear** ❄️☀️

Korea has four distinct seasons, leading to a varied wardrobe:

- **Summer:** `<speak='반바지'>` (Shorts) and `<speak='티셔츠'>` (T-shirt).
- **Winter:** `<speak='코트'>` (Coat), `<speak='패딩'>` (Padded/Puffer jacket), and `<speak='목도리'>` (Scarf).
- **Spring/Fall:** `<speak='가디건'>` (Cardigan) and `<speak='자켓'>` (Jacket).

---

### **The "Wearing" Verbs (Pro Tip)** 💡

Unlike English where we just "put on" clothes, Korean uses different verbs depending on the body part:

1. **Body (shirts, pants):** Use **`<speak='입다'>`** (Ip-da).
2. **Feet (shoes, socks):** Use **`<speak='신다'>`** (Sin-da).
3. **Head (hats, glasses):** Use **`<speak='쓰다'>`** (Sseu-da).
4. **Hands/Accessories (gloves, rings):** Use **`<speak='끼다'>`** (Kki-da).

---

### **Cultural Tips** 👗

1. **Hanbok (`<speak='한복'>`):** Traditional Korean clothing. **한** (Korean) + **복** (Clothing).
2. **Free Size:** Many street shops sell clothes in "Free Size," which is a one-size-fits-all (usually a standard Medium).
3. **Indoor Shoes:** Shoes are strictly for outdoors. Indoors, people wear **`<speak='슬리퍼'>`** (Slippers) or just socks. Always keep your socks hole-free!

householdItems.title: Household Items and Rooms
householdItems.content:
### **Rooms in the House: The 'Bang' & 'Sil' Keys** 🏠

Most room names end in **방** (room) or **실** (chamber/office). The prefix tells you the room's function:

| Room | Korean | Logic (Masterkey) |
|:---:|---|---|
| Bedroom | `<speak='침실'>` | **침** (Sleep) + **실** (Room) |
| Living Room | `<speak='거실'>` | **거** (Reside) + **실** (Room) |
| Kitchen | `<speak='주방'>` | **주** (Cook) + **방** (Room) |
| Bathroom | `<speak='화장실'>` | **화장** (Makeup/Toilette) + **실** (Room) |
| Laundry Room | `<speak='세탁실'>` | **세탁** (Wash) + **실** (Room) |

---

### **Common Household Items** 🛋️

| Item | Korean | Item | Korean |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| Bed | `<speak='침대'>` | Refrigerator | `<speak='냉장고'>` |
| Desk | `<speak='책상'>` | TV | `<speak='텔레비전'>` |
| Chair | `<speak='의자'>` | Mirror | `<speak='거울'>` |
| Window | `<speak='창문'>` | Door | `<speak='문'>` |

---

### **Masterkey: '기' (Gi) - Machines** ⚙️

The suffix **기** refers to a device or machine. If a word ends in **기**, it’s usually an appliance:

- **Wash** (`<speak='세탁'>`) + **Machine** = **`<speak='세탁기'>`** (Washing Machine)
- **Fan** (`<speak='선풍'>`) + **Machine** = **`<speak='선풍기'>`** (Electric Fan)
- **Air** + **Clean** + **Machine** = **`<speak='공기청정기'>`** (Air Purifier)

---

### **Pro Tips** 💡

1. **The Floor Culture:** In Korea, the floor (**`<speak='바닥'>`**) is a living space. Many people still eat or sleep on the floor thanks to the underfloor heating system called *Ondol*.
2. **Recycling is Mandatory:** Korea takes trash separation very seriously. You must use specific government-standard trash bags (**`<speak='쓰레기 봉투'>`**) for different types of waste.
3. **Entryway:** Every Korean home has a dedicated area at the entrance to leave shoes, called the *Hyeongwan* (**`<speak='현관'>`**). Never step past this line with shoes on!

schoolSupplies.title: Study and Technology
schoolSupplies.content:
### **The Logic of Tools** 🎒

Korean tool names aren't random; they are classified by their functional essence. To master study and tech items, look at two fundamental Hanja roots: **`<speak='통'>`** (Container) and **`<speak='기'>`** (Machine/Instrument).

#### **Masterkey: 통 (Tong - Container)**
This root indicates anything that holds something inside:
- **`<speak='연필'>`** (Pencil) + **통** = **`<speak='필통'>`** (Pencil case).
- **`<speak='쓰레기'>`** (Trash) + **통** = **`<speak='쓰레기통'>`** (Trash can).
- **`<speak='물'>`** (Water) + **통** = **`<speak='물통'>`** (Water bottle/Canteen).
- **`<speak='필름'>`** (Film) + **통** = **`<speak='필름통'>`** (Film canister).

#### **Masterkey: 기 (Gi - Machine/Tool)**
Found in almost every electronic and mechanical device:
- **`<speak='계산'>`** (Calculation) + **기** = **`<speak='계산기'>`** (Calculator).
- **`<speak='복사'>`** (Copy) + **기** = **`<speak='복사기'>`** (Copier).
- **`<speak='녹음'>`** (Recording) + **기** = **`<speak='녹음기'>`** (Recorder).
- The word for machine itself is **`<speak='기계'>`**.

#### **Modern Digital Life**
| Item | Korean | Origin/Usage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Laptop | `<speak='노트북'>` | Konglish: "Notebook" |
| Tablet | `<speak='태블릿'>` | Student essential |
| Internet | `<speak='인터넷'>` | Backbone of Korean life |
| Cell phone | `<speak='핸드폰'>` | Konglish: "Hand phone" |
| Charger | `<speak='충전기'>` | Root **기** (machine) |
| Mouse | `<speak='마우스'>` | English phonetics |

> **Gold Tip:** Stationery culture (**`<speak='문구'>`**) in Korea is immense. Shops like *Kyobo Artbox* are design temples where every pen and pencil case is designed to maximize aesthetics and productivity.

emotions.title: Feelings and Emotions
emotions.content:
### **The Grammar of the Heart** ❤️

In Korean, most emotions aren't nouns, but **Descriptive Verbs** (adjectives). The suffix **`<speak='하다'>`** (to do/be) is the engine that turns abstract concepts into mental states.

#### **Common Psychological States**
- **`<speak='행복하다'>`**: To be happy (long-term).
- **`<speak='슬프다'>`**: To be sad.
- **`<speak='기쁘다'>`**: To be glad (short-term).
- **`<speak='화나다'>`**: To be angry (literally: "fire is born").
- **`<speak='무섭다'>`**: To be scared.
- **`<speak='심심하다'>`**: To be bored (nothing to do).
- **`<speak='지루하다'>`**: To be tedious (something causing boredom).

#### **Feelings vs. Observations** 👁️
A vital rule: You can only state your own feelings with certainty. For others, you must use a form indicating observation (**-어/아하다**):
- **Me**: `<speak='저는 슬퍼요'>` (I am sad).
- **Them**: `<speak='그는 슬퍼해요'>` (He acts/seems sad).

#### **Vocabulary of Connection**
| Korean | Meaning | Nuance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `<speak='사랑하다'>` | To love | Direct action verb |
| `<speak='좋아하다'>` | To like | Literally: "to do the good" |
| `<speak='설레다'>` | Excitement | Butterflies or racing heart |
| `<speak='그립다'>` | To miss | For things/places/past |
| `<speak='보고 싶다'>` | I miss you | Literally: "want to see" (for people) |

> **Gold Tip:** The concept of **`<speak='정'>`** (Jeong) is the foundation of Korean relationships. It’s not just love or friendship, but a deep, invisible bond that develops through shared time and experiences, creating a sense of mutual responsibility.

weather.title: Weather and Atmosphere
weather.content:
### **Reading the Skies** 🌤️

Weather in Korea is dramatic and changes the landscape completely. To talk about weather, we use base terms like **`<speak='비'>`** (Rain) and **`<speak='눈'>`** (Snow/Eye) as puzzle pieces.

#### **States of the Sky**
- **`<speak='맑다'>`**: Clear / Sunny.
- **`<speak='흐리다'>`**: Cloudy / Overcast.
- **`<speak='화창하다'>`**: Radiant / Splendid day.
- **`<speak='습하다'>`**: Humid (common in summer).
- **`<speak='건조하다'>`**: Dry (common in winter).

#### **Natural Phenomena**
- **`<speak='비가 오다'>`**: To rain.
- **`<speak='눈이 오다'>`**: To snow.
- **`<speak='바람이 불다'>`**: To blow (wind).
- **`<speak='안개가 끼다'>`**: To be foggy.
- **`<speak='천둥 번개'>`**: Thunder and lightning.

#### **Rain and Snow Nuances**
| Type | Korean | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Drizzle | `<speak='이슬비'>` | Dew-like light rain |
| Shower | `<speak='소나기'>` | Sudden heavy rain |
| Monsoon | `<speak='장마'>` | Summer rainy season |
| First Snow | `<speak='첫눈'>` | Romantic cultural event |
| Blizzard | `<speak='눈보라'>` | Snow with strong winds |

#### **Yellow Dust Warning**
In spring, you will hear about **`<speak='황사'>`** (Yellow Dust) and **`<speak='미세먼지'>`** (Fine Dust). These are particles from the Gobi Desert that force everyone to wear protective masks.

> **Gold Tip:** Koreans are obsessed with forecasts due to how fast seasons shift. The term **`<speak='일교차'>`** (daily temp range) is used to warn you when mornings are cold but afternoons are hot, suggesting you carry a light jacket.

daysOfWeek.title: Days of the Week
daysOfWeek.content:
### **Elemental Cycle** 📅

Korean day names follow the philosophy of the five elements plus the Sun and Moon. All end with the Masterkey **`<speak='요일'>`** (Yo-il - Day of the Week).

| Element | Meaning | Day Name |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **月** (Wol) | Moon | **`<speak='월요일'>`** (Monday) |
| **火** (Hwa) | Fire | **`<speak='화요일'>`** (Tuesday) |
| **水** (Su) | Water | **`<speak='수요일'>`** (Wednesday) |
| **木** (Mok) | Tree | **`<speak='목요일'>`** (Thursday) |
| **金** (Geum) | Gold | **`<speak='금요일'>`** (Friday) |
| **土** (To) | Earth | **`<speak='토요일'>`** (Saturday) |
| **日** (Il) | Sun | **`<speak='일요일'>`** (Sunday) |

#### **Planning Terms**
- **`<speak='평일'>`**: Weekdays (Mon-Fri).
- **`<speak='주말'>`**: Weekend.
- **`<speak='그저께'>`**: Day before yesterday.
- **`<speak='모레'>`**: Day after tomorrow.

> **Gold Tip:** The term **`<speak='불금'>`** (Bul-geum) literally means "Fire Friday." It’s the equivalent of "T.G.I.F.," where the intensity of the Korean work week explodes into a night of food and drinks to burn off accumulated stress.

months.title: Months and Dates
months.content:
### **The Number Calendar** 🗓️

Unlike Western languages, Korean has no unique names for months. They are mathematical: **Sino-Korean Number + `<speak='월'>`** (Month).

#### **The Sequence**
- **`<speak='일월'>`**: January (Month 1)
- **`<speak='이월'>`**: February (Month 2)
- **`<speak='삼월'>`**: March (Month 3)
- **`<speak='사월'>`**: April (Month 4)
- **`<speak='오월'>`**: May (Month 5)
- **`<speak='유월'>`**: June (Month 6 - *Note: 'k' drops for flow*)
- **`<speak='시월'>`**: October (Month 10 - *Note: 'p' drops*)
- **`<speak='십이월'>`**: December (Month 12)

#### **Building a Full Date**
Korean order is descending: **Year + Month + Day**.
- **Year**: `<speak='년'>` (Nyeon).
- **Month**: `<speak='월'>` (Wol).
- **Day**: `<speak='일'>` (Il).

**Example: August 15th, 2024**
`<speak='이천이십사년 팔월 오일'>`.

> **Gold Tip:** To ask "What's today's date?", we use **`<speak='오늘이 며칠이에요?'>`**. Note that for "which day" we don't say *Myeot-il*, but **`<speak='며칠'>`**, a rare spelling exception in modern Korean.

timeOfDay.title: Time and Periods
timeOfDay.content:
### **Dual Number Challenge** ⌚

Telling time in Korean is a mental exercise, as it requires using two number systems simultaneously in the same sentence.

#### **The Golden Rule**
- **Hours**: Use **Native** Numbers + **`<speak='시'>`** (Si).
- **Minutes**: Use **Sino** Numbers + **`<speak='분'>`** (Bun).
- **Seconds**: Use **Sino** Numbers + **`<speak='초'>`** (Cho).

**Example: 05:25**
- 5 (Native): `<speak='다섯'>` → **`<speak='다섯 시'>`**.
- 25 (Sino): `<speak='이십오'>` → **`<speak='이십오 분'>`**.

#### **Dividing the Day**
| Period | Korean | Approx. Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dawn | `<speak='새벽'>` | 01:00 - 06:00 |
| Morning | `<speak='아침'>` | 06:00 - 11:00 |
| Before Noon | `<speak='오전'>` | AM |
| After Noon | `<speak='오후'>` | PM |
| Evening | `<speak='저녁'>` | 18:00 - 21:00 (Dinner) |
| Late Night | `<speak='밤'>` | 21:00 - 00:00 |

> **Gold Tip:** To say "Half past," we use the Masterkey **`<speak='반'>`** (Ban - Half). Instead of saying *Samsip-bun* (30 min), you can just say **`<speak='두 시 반'>`** (Two and a half). It's much more natural!

seasons.title: The Four Seasons
seasons.content:
### **Nature's Cycle** 🍂

Korea has four extremely distinct seasons, which deeply shapes food, clothes, and even the public's mood.

#### **1. Spring (`<speak='봄'>`)**
The season of **`<speak='벚꽃'>`** (cherry blossoms). The weather becomes **`<speak='따뜻하다'>`** (warm/pleasant). People go on picnics and pastels dominate fashion.

#### **2. Summer (`<speak='여름'>`)**
Extremely **`<speak='덥다'>`** (hot) and **`<speak='습하다'>`** (humid). It's the time of **`<speak='장마'>`** (monsoon season). People eat cold dishes like **`<speak='냉면'>`** (ice noodles) to survive.

#### **3. Autumn (`<speak='가을'>`)**
The favorite season for many. The air is **`<speak='시원하다'>`** (cool) and mountains are covered in **`<speak='단풍'>`** (red maple leaves). Time for **`<speak='가디건'>`** (cardigans).

#### **4. Winter (`<speak='겨울'>`)**
Cold is **`<speak='춥다'>`** (freezing) and dry. Snow (**`<speak='눈'>`**) is frequent and everyone wears the ubiquitous **`<speak='패딩'>`** (padded puffer coat).

> **Gold Tip:** Autumn is the season of reading and contemplation. If you're in Korea, go **`<speak='등산'>`** (hiking). The mountains painted in red and gold against a deep blue sky is unforgettable.

directions.title: Directions and Navigation
directions.content:
### **Navigating Space** 🧭

To find your way in Korea, you need two sets of vocab: everyday colloquial and formal Hanja-based terms found on elevator buttons and maps.

#### **Movement Terms**
- **`<speak='왼쪽'>`**: Left side.
- **`<speak='오른쪽'>`**: Right side.
- **`<speak='직진'>`**: Go straight (often used by GPS).
- **`<speak='뒤로'>`**: Backwards.
- **`<speak='건너편'>`**: Opposite side of the street.

#### **The Hanja Masterkeys (Essential!)**
| Hanja | Hangul | Meaning | Usage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **左** | **`<speak='좌'>`** (Jwa) | Left | Elevators, Manuals |
| **右** | **`<speak='우'>`** (U) | Right | Traffic signs |
| **상** | **`<speak='상'>`** (Sang) | Up | Floor signs |
| **하** | **`<speak='하'>`** (Ha) | Down | Subway exits |
| **前** | **`<speak='전'>`** (Jeon) | Front/Before | History, Direction |
| **後** | **`<speak='후'>`** (Hu) | Back/After | Time instructions |

#### **City Landmarks**
- **`<speak='지하철역'>`**: Subway station.
- **`<speak='신호등'>`**: Traffic light.
- **`<speak='횡단보도'>`**: Crosswalk.
- **`<speak='사거리'>`**: Intersection (lit: "four paths").

> **Gold Tip:** When taking a taxi, instead of giving a precise address, give the name of the nearest **`<speak='지하철역'>`** (subway station). The system is so precise that stations are the best geographic anchors.

verbConjulgationAndUnderstandingSOV.title: Verbs and SOV Logic
verbConjulgationAndUnderstandingSOV.content:
### **The Engine of Korean** 🧬

This is the most important lesson. In English, we use **SVO** (Subject-Verb-Object: "I drink water"). Korean uses **SOV** (Subject-Object-Verb): **"I water drink"**.

**The Verb is King:** It always, without exception, ends the sentence.

#### **1. Verb Anatomy**
In dictionaries, every verb ends in **`<speak='-다'>`** (dictionary form). To conjugate, discard `-다` to get the **Stem**.
- Ex: **`<speak='먹다'>`** (To eat) → Stem is **`<speak='먹'>`**.
- Ex: **`<speak='가다'>`** (To go) → Stem is **`<speak='가'>`**.

#### **2. Conjugating in the Polite Present (해요체)**
Rules depend on the stem's last vowel:
- **Rule A**: If the last vowel is **ㅏ** or **ㅗ**, add **-아요**.
  - Ex: **`<speak='가다'>`** → **`<speak='가요'>`** (I go).
  - Ex: **`<speak='보다'>`** (To see) → **`<speak='봐요'>`** (I see).
- **Rule B**: For any other vowel, add **-어요**.
  - Ex: **`<speak='먹다'>`** → **`<speak='먹어요'>`** (I eat).
  - Ex: **`<speak='마시다'>`** (To drink) → **`<speak='마셔요'>`** (I drink).
- **Rule C**: Verbs ending in **`<speak='하다'>`** (To do) always become **`<speak='해요'>`**.

#### **3. Tense Markers**
- **Past**: Add **-았어요** or **-었어요**.
  - Ex: **`<speak='먹었어요'>`** (Ate).
- **Future**: Add **-ㄹ/을 거예요**.
  - Ex: **`<speak='먹을 거예요'>`** (Will eat).

#### **Sentence Examples**
1. **Present**: `<speak='저는 학교에 가요'>` (I go to school).
2. **Object**: `<speak='동생이 우유를 마셔요'>` (The younger sibling drinks milk).
3. **Past**: `<speak='어제 영화를 봤어요'>` (I saw a movie yesterday).
4. **Future**: `<speak='내일 친구를 만날 거예요'>` (I will meet a friend tomorrow).

> **Gold Tip:** Verbs don't change based on person. **`<speak='먹어요'>`** can mean I eat, you eat, or they eat. Context and subject particles define the actor!

adjectives.title: Descriptive Verbs
adjectives.content:
### **Adjectives are Verbs** 📌

In Korean, adjectives are **Descriptive Verbs**. They conjugate, have tenses, and most importantly: **don't need the verb "to be"**.

#### **Adjectives as Verbs (Sentence End)**
If you want to say "The weather is good", the adjective **`<speak='좋다'>`** (to be good) does the work:
- **Korean**: `<speak='날씨가 좋아요'>` (Weather good-is).
- There is no word for "is". The adjective *is* the state.

#### **Adjectives as Modifiers (Before Noun)**
To say "A good day", transform the verb into an adjective using **-ㄴ/은**:
- **Stem**: `<speak='좋'>` + **은** = **`<speak='좋은'>`**.
- **Phrase**: `<speak='좋은 날'>` (Good day).

#### **Opposite Pairs**
| Quality | Korean | Opposite | Korean |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Big | `<speak='크다'>` | Small | `<speak='작다'>` |
| Expensive | `<speak='비싸다'>` | Cheap | `<speak='싸다'>` |
| Hot (Weather) | `<speak='덥다'>` | Cold (Weather) | `<speak='춥다'>` |
| Easy | `<speak='쉽다'>` | Difficult | `<speak='어렵다'>` |
| Fast | `<speak='빠르다'>` | Slow | `<speak='느리다'>` |

> **Gold Tip:** Be careful with temperature! One pair is for weather (**`<speak='덥다/춥다'>`**) and another for touch/objects (**`<speak='뜨겁다/차갑다'>`**). Using *Tteugeopda* for weather sounds like the air is literally burning your skin!

banmal.title: Banmal and Etiquette
banmal.content:
### **The Hierarchy of Respect** 🧢

Korean is a socially stratified language. **Banmal** (**`<speak='반말'>`**) means "half-speech"-the casual level. Using it correctly shows cultural depth; using it wrong can end a friendship instantly.

#### **How it works**
To speak in Banmal, drop the polite **-요**.
- **Polite**: `<speak='안녕하세요'>` (Hello) → **Casual**: `<speak='안녕'>`.
- **Polite**: `<speak='고마워요'>` (Thanks) → **Casual**: `<speak='고마워'>`.

#### **Social Danger** ⚠️
Never use Banmal with:
1. Elders (even just 1 year older!).
2. Strangers on the street.
3. Coworkers or bosses.

#### **When is it allowed?**
- With very close friends (same birth year).
- With younger siblings or children.
- When an elder gives explicit permission.

> **Gold Tip:** There is a ritual for this. An older friend might say: **`<speak='말 놓으셔도 돼요'>`** (You can drop the honorifics). Only then are you free to be casual. If in doubt, stay with **-요**!

particles.title: Particles and Functions
particles.content:
### **Grammatical Signposts** ✨

Without particles, Korean is just a list of words. They are suffixes attached to nouns to say: "Hey, this word here is the Subject!".

#### **The Big Three Families**

1. **Topic (`<speak='은/는'>`)**: Defines the subject or contrast.
   - Consonant end: **-은**. Vowel end: **-는**.
   - Ex: **`<speak='저는...'>`** (As for me...).

2. **Subject (`<speak='이/가'>`)**: Focuses on WHO does the action.
   - Consonant end: **-이**. Vowel end: **-가**.
   - Ex: **`<speak='내가 했어요'>`** (I specifically did it).

3. **Object (`<speak='을/를'>`)**: Focuses on WHAT receives action.
   - Consonant end: **-을**. Vowel end: **-를**.
   - Ex: **`<speak='밥을 먹어요'>`** (Eating rice).

#### **Location and Time**
- **`<speak='~에'>`**: Static location (to be at) or time.
- **`<speak='~에서'>`**: Dynamic location (action happening at).

> **Gold Tip:** In casual speech, Koreans often drop object particles (*Bap meogeoyo* instead of *Babeul meogeoyo*). However, topic and subject particles are vital to avoid confusing who is doing what!

hobbies.title: Leisure and Hobbies
hobbies.content:
### **The Culture of Doing** 🔀

Most hobbies follow a simple logic: **Noun + `<speak='하다'>`** (To do). If you know the sport's name, you know the verb.

#### **Action Hobbies**
- **`<speak='축구하다'>`**: To play soccer.
- **`<speak='농구하다'>`**: To play basketball.
- **`<speak='수영하다'>`**: To swim.
- **`<speak='게임하다'>`**: To play games.
- **`<speak='요리하다'>`**: To cook.

#### **Arts and Creativity**
| Hobby | Korean | Verb used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Singing | `<speak='노래하다'>` | Doing song |
| Drawing | `<speak='그림을 그리다'>` | Pulling a drawing |
| Instrument | `<speak='악기를 연주하다'>` | Performing instrument |
| Photo | `<speak='사진을 찍다'>` | Stamping a photo |

#### **The 'Bangs' (Hobby Rooms)**
Korea is famous for specialized rooms for hobbies:
- **`<speak='노래방'>`**: Karaoke.
- **`<speak='PC방'>`**: High-end internet cafes.
- **`<speak='만화방'>`**: Comic book reading rooms.

> **Gold Tip:** When Koreans ask **`<speak='취미가 뭐예요?'>`** (What is your hobby?), it's not just small talk. Shared hobbies are the basis for forming lifelong friend groups in Korean society.

appearance.title: Physical Appearance
appearance.content:
### **Describing People** 🎨

Describing someone in Korean requires full phrases with descriptive verbs. We rarely use standalone adjectives.

#### **Physique and Stature**
- **`<speak='키가 크다'>`**: To be tall (lit: "height is big").
- **`<speak='키가 작다'>`**: To be short.
- **`<speak='날씬하다'>`**: To be slim/fit.
- **`<speak='뚱뚱하다'>`**: To be fat (Caution: can be offensive!).

#### **Hair and Style**
- **`<speak='머리가 길다'>`**: Long hair.
- **`<speak='머리가 짧다'>`**: Short hair.
- **`<speak='머리 색'>`**: Hair color.

#### **Common Compliments**
| Compliment | Korean | Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pretty | `<speak='예쁘다'>` | Women, objects, flowers |
| Beautiful | `<speak='아름답다'>` | Scenery, nature |
| Handsome | `<speak='잘생겼다'>` | Men (lit: "born well") |
| Cool | `<speak='멋있다'>` | Style and attitude |
| Cute | `<speak='귀엽다'>` | Kids, animals, acting cute |

> **Gold Tip:** Appearance is a sign of self-respect and care for others. You'll see an immense concentration of **`<speak='미용실'>`** (hair salons). The term **`<speak='관리'>`** (management/care) describes the habit of caring for one's skin and body.

dailyRoutine.title: Daily Routine
dailyRoutine.content:
### **The Flow of the Day** 🔄

To describe your day, master the sequence of action verbs and connect them with time markers using the particle **`<speak='에'>`**.

#### **Chronological Sequence**
1. **`<speak='일어나다'>`**: To wake up / Get up.
2. **`<speak='세수하다'>`**: To wash one's face.
3. **`<speak='샤워하다'>`**: To shower.
4. **`<speak='아침을 먹다'>`**: To eat breakfast.
5. **`<speak='출근하다'>`**: To go to work (Entering office).
6. **`<speak='일하다'>`**: To work.
7. **`<speak='퇴근하다'>`**: To leave work / Go home.
8. **`<speak='운동하다'>`**: To exercise.
9. **`<speak='자다'>`**: To sleep.

#### **Time Markers**
| Time | Korean | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| At X o'clock | **[Time] 에** | `<speak='일곱 시에 일어나요'>` |
| In the morning | `<speak='아침에'>` | `<speak='아침에 운동해요'>` |
| In the afternoon | `<speak='오후에'>` | `<speak='오후에 일해요'>` |
| At night | `<speak='밤에'>` | `<speak='밤에 자요'>` |

> **Gold Tip:** Work is central. The term **`<speak='야근'>`** (Ya-geun - night work/overtime) is unfortunately common. If you meet a tired friend, ask if they did *yageun* yesterday!

basicQuestions.title: The 6 Questions
basicQuestions.content:
### **Conversation Keys** ❓

These six words are the keys to unlocking any interaction. In Korean, questions keep the same SOV order, simply swapping the object or subject for the interrogative word.

#### **Base Interrogatives**
- **`<speak='누구'>`**: Who. (Ex: `<speak='누구예요?'>` - Who is it?).
- **`<speak='뭐 / 무엇'>`**: What. (Ex: `<speak='뭐 해요?'>` - What are you doing?).
- **`<speak='어디'>`**: Where. (Ex: `<speak='어디 가요?'>` - Where are you going?).
- **`<speak='언제'>`**: When. (Ex: `<speak='언제 만나요?'>` - When do we meet?).
- **`<speak='왜'>`**: Why.
- **`<speak='어떻게'>`**: How. (Ex: `<speak='어떻게 가요?'>` - How do I get there?).

#### **The "You" Taboo** ⚠️
Never use "You" (**`<speak='너'>`** or **`<speak='당신'>`**) in questions. It sounds aggressive. Instead:
1. Omit the subject (if looking at them).
2. Use Name + **`<speak='씨'>`** (Mr/Ms).
3. Use their title (Teacher, Manager, etc).

> **Gold Tip:** "What" (**`<speak='무엇'>`**) is often shortened to **`<speak='뭐'>`**. "Why" (**`<speak='왜'>`**) can be used alone as a direct answer, but be careful not to sound too blunt!

connectors.title: Connectors and Flow
connectors.content:
### **The Speech Bridges** 🔗

To stop speaking in isolated fragments and start flowing naturally, you need connectors. They link what you just said to what comes next.

#### **Pillars of Cohesion**
1. **`<speak='그리고'>`** (And): Adds info.
   - *Ex: I like apples. **AND** I like bananas.*
2. **`<speak='하지만 / 그렇지만'>`** (But): Shows opposition.
   - *Ex: I want to go. **BUT** I'm tired.*
3. **`<speak='그래서'>`** (So/Therefore): Shows result.
   - *Ex: It rained. **SO** I didn't go out.*
4. **`<speak='그러면'>`** (Then/In that case): Sets a condition.
   - *Ex: Are you hungry? **THEN** let's eat.*
5. **`<speak='그런데'>`** (However/By the way): Changes subject or contrasts.

#### **Real-World Shortening**
Efficiency is everything. You'll rarely hear *Geureonde* in a cafe; you'll hear **`<speak='근데'>`**. 
- **`<speak='근데 있잖아...'>`**: "But you know what..." (Universal way to start gossip or pivot a talk).

> **Gold Tip:** **`<speak='그러니까'>`** (Geureonikka) is used to emphasize an obvious conclusion. It's like "That's exactly why...". Use it sparingly to not sound impatient!

hasipsioche.title: Formal Hasipsioche
hasipsioche.content:
### **The Voice of Authority** 👑

**Hasipsioche** (**`<speak='하십시오체'>`**) is the highest formal level in modern Korean. It's the language of news, military, public speeches, and high-end department stores.

#### **Mechanical Conjugation**
It's the easiest level to learn due to few irregularities. Take the verb stem:
- **Vowel ending**: Add **-ㅂ니다**.
  - Ex: **`<speak='가다'>`** → **`<speak='갑니다'>`** (I go).
- **Consonant ending**: Add **-습니다**.
  - Ex: **`<speak='먹다'>`** → **`<speak='먹습니다'>`** (I eat).

#### **Asking Questions**
Simply swap *-nida* for **-nikka?**:
- **`<speak='갑니까?'>`** (Are you going?).
- **`<speak='먹습니까?'>`** (Are you eating?).

#### **When to use it**
- **Presentations**: Speaking to a group.
- **First Meetings**: In very formal work environments.
- **Announcements**: Subways, airports, and radio.

> **Gold Tip:** You'll hear this the moment you walk into any restaurant: **`<speak='어서 오십시오!'>`** (Welcome!). While daily life is mostly *-yo* level, knowing Hasipsioche is vital for understanding announcements and showing peak respect.

geography.title: Geography and Nature
geography.content:
### **Mapping the Peninsula** 🌍

Korean geography is defined by mountains. Roots tell the story of the land, as they name almost every city and province.

#### **Topography and Nature**
- **`<speak='산'>`** (San): Mountain. (Ex: **`<speak='한라산'>`**).
- **`<speak='강'>`** (Gang): River. (Ex: **`<speak='한강'>`**).
- **`<speak='계곡'>`** (Gyegok): Valley.
- **`<speak='평야'>`** (Pyeong-ya): Plain.
- **`<speak='바다'>`** (Bada): Sea / Ocean.
- **`<speak='섬'>`** (Seom): Island. Becomes **`<speak='도'>`** in names (Ex: **`<speak='제주도'>`**).
- **`<speak='호수'>`** (Hosu): Lake.
- **`<speak='숲'>`** (Sup): Forest.

#### **Administrative Divisions**
| Root | Hangul | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **市** | **`<speak='시'>`** (Si) | City (Ex: Seoul-si) |
| **道** | **`<speak='도'>`** (Do) | Province / Way |
| **區** | **`<speak='구'>`** (Gu) | District (Ex: Gangnam-gu) |
| **洞** | **`<speak='동'>`** (Dong) | Neighborhood |

> **Gold Tip:** Korea is 70% mountainous. This explains why **`<speak='등산'>`** (hiking) is the national hobby. Even in Seoul, you're minutes from peaks like Bukhansan. To know Korea, climb a mountain!

professions.title: Professions
professions.content:
### **The World of Work** 💼

In Korean, professions are grouped by suffixes that describe the nature of their expertise. Understanding these suffixes helps you deduce a job title just by its ending.

#### **1. Licensed Specialist (-사)**
Indicates occupations requiring government certification or specific academic degrees:
- **`<speak='의사'>`**: Doctor.
- **`<speak='변호사'>`**: Lawyer.
- **`<speak='판사'>`**: Judge.
- **`<speak='검사'>`**: Prosecutor.
- **`<speak='요리사'>`**: Chef/Cook.
- **`<speak='간호사'>`**: Nurse.
- **`<speak='약사'>`**: Pharmacist.

#### **2. Corporate Employee (-원)**
Indicates membership in an organization or institution:
- **`<speak='회사원'>`**: Office worker.
- **`<speak='공무원'>`**: Civil servant / Public official.
- **`<speak='은행원'>`**: Banker.
- **`<speak='승무원'>`**: Flight attendant.
- **`<speak='판매원'>`**: Salesperson.

#### **3. Creative & Artist (-가)**
Indicates someone dedicated to an art or intellectual creation:
- **`<speak='작가'>`**: Writer.
- **`<speak='화가'>`**: Painter.
- **`<speak='음악가'>`**: Musician.
- **`<speak='만화가'>`**: Cartoonist / Manhwaga.
- **`<speak='무용가'>`**: Professional dancer.

#### **4. Officers & Manual Trades (Exceptions)**
Not everything follows the three rules above. There are specific suffixes for officers and manual labor:
- **Officers (`-관`)**: **`<speak='경찰관'>`** (Police officer), **`<speak='소방관'>`** (Firefighter), **`<speak='외교관'>`** (Diplomat).
- **Manual Labor (`-부`)**: **`<speak='농부'>`** (Farmer), **`<speak='어부'>`** (Fisherman), **`<speak='광부'>`** (Miner).
- **Others**: **`<speak='선생님'>`** (Teacher), **`<speak='가수'>`** (Singer), **`<speak='군인'>`** (Soldier).

> **Gold Tip:** Never call someone just by their job title. Always add **`<speak='님'>`** (Nim) to be respectful. E.g., **`<speak='의사 선생님'>`** or **`<speak='기사님'>`** (for drivers/technicians).

toysGames.title: Games and Fun
toysGames.content:
### **From Tradition to E-Sports** 🎮

Korea is world-famous for gaming, but its culture spans from thousand-year-old street games to cutting-edge digital competitions.

#### **Basic Toys**
- **`<speak='장난감'>`**: Toy.
- **`<speak='인형'>`**: Doll or plushie.
- **`<speak='공'>`**: Ball.
- **`<speak='퍼즐'>`**: Puzzle.
- **`<speak='블록'>`**: Building blocks (Lego).

#### **Traditional Games (Minsok-nori)**
These are often played during holidays like Chuseok or Seollal:
- **`<speak='윷놀이'>`**: Board game using four wooden sticks.
- **`<speak='제기차기'>`**: Kicking a shuttlecock with feet.
- **`<speak='투호'>`**: Throwing arrows into a narrow-necked jar.
- **`<speak='연날리기'>`**: Kite flying.
- **`<speak='공기놀이'>`**: Game played with five small pebbles.

#### **Strategy & Board Games**
- **`<speak='바둑'>`**: The famous strategy game 'Go'.
- **`<speak='장기'>`**: Korean chess.
- **`<speak='고스톱'>`**: Korean card game played with **`<speak='화투'>`** (flower cards).

#### **Modern Gaming Culture**
- **`<speak='게임'>`**: Game (general).
- **`<speak='컴퓨터 게임'>`**: PC game.
- **`<speak='모바일 게임'>`**: Mobile game.
- **`<speak='PC방'>`**: High-end gaming center.

> **Gold Tip:** The **`<speak='PC방'>`** is a social hub in Korea. It's not just about gaming; you can order delicious ramen and snacks delivered right to your desk. It’s the center of Korean digital youth culture.

transport.title: Transport
transport.content:
### **Moving through the Peninsula** 🚀

Korea has one of the world's most efficient transport systems. When talking about transport, it's important to distinguish technical terms from colloquial ones.

#### **Land Vehicles**
- **`<speak='차'>`**: Car (colloquial and short form).
- **`<speak='자동차'>`**: Automobile (technical/formal term).
- **`<speak='버스'>`**: Bus.
- **`<speak='마을버스'>`**: Village bus (green, short routes).
- **`<speak='택시'>`**: Taxi.
- **`<speak='자전거'>`**: Bicycle.
- **`<speak='오토바이'>`**: Motorcycle / Scooter.
- **`<speak='트럭'>`**: Truck.
- **`<speak='구급차'>`**: Ambulance (lit: "urgent rescue car").

#### **Rails and Air**
- **`<speak='지하철'>`**: Subway.
- **`<speak='기차'>`**: Train.
- **`<speak='KTX'>`**: Korean high-speed rail.
- **`<speak='비행기'>`**: Airplane.
- **`<speak='헬리콥터'>`**: Helicopter.
- **`<speak='케이블카'>`**: Cable car (famous in Busan and Namsan).

#### **Water and Hubs**
- **`<speak='배'>`**: Boat or Ship.
- **`<speak='잠수함'>`**: Submarine.
- **`<speak='공항'>`**: Airport.
- **`<speak='역'>`**: Station (train/subway).
- **`<speak='정류장'>`**: Stop (bus).
- **`<speak='터미널'>`**: Bus terminal.

> **Gold Tip:** The **T-Money** card is mandatory for life in Korea. You use it for subways, buses, taxis, and even at convenience stores. Transfers between buses and subways are free for a certain time limit if you tap your card!

placesAndBuildings.title: Places and Buildings
placesAndBuildings.content:
### **Exploring the City** 🏙️

Korea blends ancient temples with futuristic skyscrapers. Understanding building suffixes helps you know a place's function before you even step inside.

#### **Daily Life**
- **`<speak='집'>`**: House.
- **`<speak='아파트'>`**: Apartment (where most Koreans live).
- **`<speak='편의점'>`**: Convenience store (CU, GS25).
- **`<speak='약국'>`**: Pharmacy.
- **`<speak='은행'>`**: Bank.
- **`<speak='우체국'>`**: Post office.
- **`<speak='백화점'>`**: Department store.
- **`<speak='시장'>`**: Traditional market.

#### **Education and Culture**
- **`<speak='학교'>`**: School.
- **`<speak='대학교'>`**: University.
- **`<speak='학원'>`**: Academy / Cram school (central to Korean life).
- **`<speak='도서관'>`**: Library.
- **`<speak='박물관'>`**: Museum.
- **`<speak='미술관'>`**: Art gallery.
- **`<speak='영화관'>`**: Cinema.

#### **Leisure and Health**
- **`<speak='공원'>`**: Park.
- **`<speak='놀이터'>`**: Playground.
- **`<speak='수영장'>`**: Swimming pool.
- **`<speak='헬스장'>`**: Gym.
- **`<speak='병원'>`**: Hospital.
- **`<speak='카페'>`**: Cafe.
- **`<speak='빵집'>`**: Bakery.

#### **Building Logic Suffixes**
- **`-관` (Public/Large building)**: **`<speak='체육관'>`** (Gymnasium), **`<speak='대사관'>`** (Embassy).
- **`-실` (Room/Office)**: **`<speak='교실'>`** (Classroom), **`<speak='사무실'>`** (Office).
- **`-소` (Service point)**: **`<speak='주유소'>`** (Gas station), **`<speak='파출소'>`** (Police post).

> **Gold Tip:** Korean addresses often end in **`<speak='~동'>`** (neighborhood) or **`<speak='~구'>`** (district). Gangnam is actually the "Gangnam District" (**`<speak='강남구'>`**).

koreanFood.title: Korean Cuisine
koreanFood.content:
### **A Symphony of Flavors** 🍲

Korean food is much more than just side dishes. It's a balance of fermentation, spice, and texture.

#### **Korean BBQ (Gogi-gui)**
The world-famous dining experience where you grill meat at the table:
- **`<speak='삼겹살'>`** (Samgyeopsal): Grilled pork belly.
- **`<speak='불고기'>`** (Bulgogi): Thinly sliced marinated beef.
- **`<speak='갈비'>`** (Galbi): Marinated beef ribs.

#### **Noodles and Classics**
- **`<speak='잡채'>`** (Japchae): Stir-fried glass noodles with vegetables and meat (a party favorite!).
- **`<speak='냉면'>`** (Naengmyeon): Cold buckwheat noodles, perfect for summer heat.
- **`<speak='비빔밥'>`** (Bibimbap): Mixed rice with various vegetables, meat, and red chili paste.

#### **Soup and Stew Variety**
Soups (**탕 - tang**) and stews (**찌개 - jjigae**) anchor every meal:
- **`<speak='김치찌개'>`**: Spicy Kimchi stew.
- **`<speak='된장찌개'>`**: Fermented soybean paste stew (comfort food staple).
- **`<speak='삼계탕'>`**: Whole ginseng chicken soup (eaten for stamina and health).

#### **Street Food (Bunsik)**
- **`<speak='떡볶이'>`** (Tteokbokki): Spicy rice cakes in a sweet-chili sauce.
- **`<speak='김밥'>`** (Gimbap): Seaweed rice rolls seasoned with sesame oil.

> **Gold Tip:** Table etiquette is vital! Never lift your rice bowl off the table, and always wait for the elders to pick up their chopsticks first. And remember, **`<speak='반찬'>`** (Banchan) are meant to be shared!

culturalHolidays.title: Holidays and Traditions
culturalHolidays.content:
### **Celebrating the Lunar Cycle** 🎆

Korean holidays are deeply rooted in Confucian traditions, family reunions, and ancestral respect.

#### **Lunar New Year (Seollal)**
The most important holiday. Koreans return to their hometowns for sacred rites:
- **`<speak='세배'>`**: A formal deep bow performed to elders to show respect.
- **`<speak='떡국'>`**: Eating rice cake soup. It's culturally said you don't grow a year older until you finish your bowl!
- **`<speak='윷놀이'>`**: A traditional board game involving the tossing of four wooden sticks.

#### **The Harvest Moon (Chuseok)**
The Korean Thanksgiving. A time to celebrate the harvest and honor ancestors:
- **`<speak='송편'>`**: Half-moon shaped rice cakes filled with sweet honey, sesame, or beans.
- **`<speak='차례'>`** (Charye): Ancestral memorial rites where elaborate tables of food are offered.
- **`<speak='강강술래'>`**: A traditional circular dance performed by women under the light of the full moon.

#### **Traditional Clothing**
- **`<speak='한복'>`** (Hanbok): The beautiful, vibrantly colored traditional attire worn during these festivals.

> **Gold Tip:** During Seollal, elders give children **`<speak='세뱃돈'>`** (New Year's money) after receiving a bow. It’s the favorite part of the year for young Koreans, often funding their hobbies for months!

ㄱ.base:
**기역** · velar consonant, plain (unaspirated)

At syllable onset it sounds like a soft 'g' — <speak='가'>, <speak='기다리다'>. Not as strong as English 'k', and softer than English 'g'. No puff of air.

Korean has **three versions** of each articulation point:

- **ㄱ** · plain — <speak='가'>
- **ㅋ** · aspirated (strong breath) — <speak='카'>
- **ㄲ** · tense, no breath (fortis) — <speak='까'>

Try comparing all three in a row — the difference becomes clearer with practice!

Common words: <speak='가방'> (bag) · <speak='고기'> (meat) · <speak='학교'> (school)
ㄱ.batchim:
As batchim, ㄱ closes the syllable as an unreleased velar stop — the throat closes but no air escapes. It sounds like a silent 'k'.

When followed by a vowel, ㄱ "moves" to the next syllable:
<speak='학이'> pronounced [하기] · <speak='국어'> pronounced [구거]

Before ㅎ, they merge into ㅋ (aspiration):
<speak='착하다'> pronounced [차카다]

ㄴ.base:
**니은** · alveolar nasal 'n'

Identical to 'n' in "no" — <speak='나'>, <speak='누나'>, <speak='나라'>. One of the most stable consonants in Korean — same sound in all positions.

Common words:
- <speak='나'> (I/me)
- <speak='노래'> (song)
- <speak='나라'> (country)
- <speak='눈'> (eye / snow)
ㄴ.batchim:
As batchim, closes with nasal 'n'. Very common and easy to identify.

When followed by a vowel, ㄴ links to the next syllable:
<speak='눈이'> pronounced [누니] · <speak='반이'> pronounced [바니]

Before ㄹ, ㄴ can assimilate to ㄹ in fast speech:
<speak='신라'> pronounced [실라]

ㄷ.base:
**디귿** · alveolar stop, plain (unaspirated)

At syllable onset, sounds between 'd' and 't' — <speak='다'>, <speak='도'>. No puff of air. Plain counterpart of ㅌ.

**Alveolar trio:**
- **ㄷ** · plain — <speak='다'>
- **ㅌ** · aspirated — <speak='타'>
- **ㄸ** · tense — <speak='따'>

**Palatalization** (important rule!): When ㄷ meets ㅣ in the next syllable, it becomes ㅈ:
- 굳이 → <speak='굳이'> sounds like [구지]
- 같이 → <speak='같이'> sounds like [가치] (ㅌ → ㅊ by same rule)
ㄷ.batchim:
As batchim, ㄷ closes with an unreleased alveolar stop — muffled sound, no air.

Links to next vowel: <speak='믿어'> pronounced [미더]

Before ㅎ, merges to ㅌ: <speak='좋다'> pronounced [조타]

Before ㅣ in next syllable, palatalizes to ㅈ: <speak='굳이'> pronounced [구지]

ㄹ.base:
**리을** · the most unique consonant in Korean!

ㄹ has no direct English equivalent — it's between 'r' and 'l' depending on position.

**At syllable onset:** quick flap, like 'r' in Spanish "caro" or 'd' in English "ladder" — <speak='라'>, <speak='리'>

**Between vowels:** vibrates quickly — <speak='사람'> (person), <speak='바람'> (wind)

**Doubled (ㄹㄹ):** sounds like a long 'l' — <speak='달리다'> (to run)

Tip: don't force either 'r' or 'l' — it's a light, quick sound in between!
ㄹ.batchim:
As batchim, ㄹ clearly sounds like 'l' — tongue touches upper teeth: <speak='달'>, <speak='말'>, <speak='일'>

Links to next vowel as flap: <speak='달이'> pronounced [다리] · <speak='말이'> pronounced [마리]

Before ㄴ, the ㄴ assimilates to ㄹ: <speak='일년'> pronounced [일련]

ㅁ.base:
**미음** · bilabial nasal 'm'

Identical to 'm' in "map" — <speak='마'>, <speak='미'>, <speak='모'>. Completely stable in all positions. Easy!

Common words:
- <speak='마음'> (heart, mind)
- <speak='나무'> (tree)
- <speak='모두'> (everyone)
- <speak='무엇'> (what)
ㅁ.batchim:
As batchim, closes with bilabial nasal 'm' — lips close at syllable end: <speak='밤'> (night), <speak='봄'> (spring), <speak='이름'> (name)

Links to next vowel naturally:
<speak='이름이'> pronounced [이르미] · <speak='밤이'> pronounced [바미]

ㅂ.base:
**비읍** · bilabial stop, plain (unaspirated)

At syllable onset, sounds between 'b' and 'p' — <speak='바'>, <speak='비'>. No puff of air. Plain counterpart of ㅍ.

**Bilabial trio:**
- **ㅂ** · plain — <speak='바'>
- **ㅍ** · aspirated — <speak='파'>
- **ㅃ** · tense — <speak='빠'>

**Note:** At the very start of a sentence, ㅂ sounds slightly more like 'p'. Mid-sentence it sounds more like 'b'. In practice, it's between the two — neither fully 'b' nor 'p'!
ㅂ.batchim:
As batchim, ㅂ closes with an unreleased bilabial stop — lips close but don't pop: <speak='밥'> (rice), <speak='입'> (mouth), <speak='앞'> (front)

Links to next vowel as 'b': <speak='밥이'> pronounced [바비] · <speak='입이'> pronounced [이비]

Before ㅎ, merges to ㅍ: <speak='잡히다'> pronounced [자피다]

ㅅ.base:
**시옷** · fricative 's'

At syllable onset, sounds like 's' — <speak='사'>, <speak='소'>, <speak='수'>. Simple!

**Important exception:** Before vowels ㅣ, ㅑ, ㅕ, ㅛ, ㅠ, ㅖ, ㅒ, ㅢ, ㅅ becomes "sh" (like in "show"):
- <speak='시'> sounds [sh + i]
- <speak='시작'> (start) sounds [시장 — sh at start]
- <speak='샤워'> (shower) sounds [sh + ya]

This palatalization is automatic and instinctive for Korean speakers!
ㅅ.batchim:
As batchim, ㅅ closes like an unreleased alveolar stop (same as ㄷ-batchim class).

Links to next vowel as 's': <speak='옷이'> pronounced [오시] · <speak='맛이'> pronounced [마시]

Note: many different batchim (ㄷ, ㅅ, ㄸ, ㅈ, ㅊ, ㅌ, ㅎ) sound identical in final position — only linking to a vowel reveals the difference!

ㅇ.base:
**이응** · the most special consonant in Korean!

ㅇ has **two completely different sounds** depending on position:

**At syllable ONSET → SILENT.** Just a placeholder, showing the syllable starts with a pure vowel. No consonant sound:
- <speak='아'> = just 'a'
- <speak='이'> = just 'i'
- <speak='오'> = just 'o'

**As BATCHIM (final) → 'ng' velar nasal**, like in "ban**g**", "son**g**", "thi**ng**":
- <speak='강'> (river)
- <speak='방'> (room)
- <speak='공'> (ball)
- <speak='성'> (castle)

This distinction is fundamental to understanding the Hangul writing system!
ㅇ.batchim:
As batchim 'ng' — <speak='강'>, <speak='방'>, <speak='성'>

When followed by a vowel, the ㅇ onset of the next syllable absorbs it silently:
<speak='방이'> pronounced [바이] (the 'ng' dissolves)
<speak='강이'> pronounced [가이]

Note: in careful speech, the final 'ng' may be lightly preserved before a vowel.

ㅈ.base:
**지읒** · affricate 'j', plain (unaspirated)

Sounds like 'j' in "just" but without breath — <speak='자'>, <speak='저'>, <speak='조'>. Plain counterpart of ㅊ.

**Affricate trio:**
- **ㅈ** · plain — <speak='자'>
- **ㅊ** · aspirated — <speak='차'>
- **ㅉ** · tense — <speak='짜'>

Common words:
- <speak='저'> (I — humble form)
- <speak='자다'> (to sleep)
- <speak='좋다'> (to be good)
- <speak='진짜'> (really, truly)
ㅈ.batchim:
As batchim, ㅈ closes as unreleased alveolar stop (ㄷ group):
<speak='낮'> (day), <speak='빛'> (light)

Links to next vowel as 'j': <speak='낮이'> pronounced [나지]

ㅊ.base:
**치읓** · affricate 'ch', aspirated

Sounds like 'ch' in "church" with a strong puff of air — <speak='차'>, <speak='처'>, <speak='초'>. Aspirated form of ㅈ. The breath is clearly noticeable!

Test: hold your hand in front of your mouth and say <speak='차'> — you should feel air. Then say <speak='자'> — almost nothing.

Common words:
- <speak='차'> (tea / car)
- <speak='책'> (book)
- <speak='처음'> (for the first time)
- <speak='친구'> (friend)
ㅊ.batchim:
As batchim, ㅊ closes as unreleased stop (ㄷ group): <speak='꽃'> (flower)

Links to next vowel as 'ch': <speak='꽃이'> pronounced [꼬치]

ㅋ.base:
**키읔** · velar stop 'k', aspirated

Sounds like 'k' in "king" with a strong puff of air — <speak='카'>, <speak='커'>, <speak='코'>. Aspirated form of ㄱ. The difference from ㄱ is the breath you feel coming out.

Remember the trio: **ㄱ** (soft) / **ㅋ** (with breath) / **ㄲ** (tense)

Common words:
- <speak='카메라'> (camera)
- <speak='커피'> (coffee)
- <speak='코'> (nose)
- <speak='크다'> (to be big)
ㅋ.batchim: As batchim, same class as ㄱ. Links to next vowel: <speak='부엌이'> pronounced [부어키]

ㅌ.base:
**티읕** · alveolar stop 't', aspirated

Sounds like 't' in "top" with a strong puff of air — <speak='타'>, <speak='터'>, <speak='토'>. Aspirated form of ㄷ.

**Palatalization** (same rule as ㄷ): When ㅌ meets ㅣ in the next syllable, it becomes ㅊ:
- 같이 → <speak='같이'> sounds [가치]
- 밭이 → <speak='밭이'> sounds [바치]

**Alveolar trio:** ㄷ (plain) · **ㅌ** (aspirated) · ㄸ (tense)
ㅌ.batchim: As batchim, closes in ㄷ group. Palatalizes before ㅣ: <speak='밭이'> pronounced [바치]

ㅍ.base:
**피읖** · bilabial stop 'p', aspirated

Sounds like 'p' with a strong puff of air — <speak='파'>, <speak='퍼'>, <speak='포'>. Aspirated form of ㅂ.

**Bilabial trio:** ㅂ (plain) · **ㅍ** (aspirated) · ㅃ (tense)

Common words:
- <speak='파'> (green onion / wave)
- <speak='피'> (blood)
- <speak='편의점'> (convenience store)
- <speak='피자'> (pizza)
ㅍ.batchim: As batchim, closes in ㅂ group. Links to next vowel as 'p': <speak='앞이'> pronounced [아피]

ㅎ.base:
**히읗** · glottal fricative 'h'

At syllable onset, sounds like 'h' in "happy" — <speak='하'>, <speak='히'>, <speak='호'>. Simple!

**Special behaviors:**

1. **Weakens between vowels or after nasals**: 많이 → <speak='많이'> sounds [마니] (ㅎ nearly disappears)

2. **Causes aspiration** when next to ㄱ, ㄷ, ㅂ, ㅈ — they fuse into ㅋ, ㅌ, ㅍ, ㅊ:
- 좋다 → <speak='좋다'> sounds [조타] (ㅎ + ㄷ = ㅌ)
- 입학 → <speak='입학'> sounds [이팍] (ㅂ + ㅎ = ㅍ)
- 백화점 → <speak='백화점'> sounds [배콰점] (ㄱ + ㅎ = ㅋ)
ㅎ.batchim:
As batchim, ㅎ silently disappears when followed by a vowel — it "evaporates":
<speak='놓아'> pronounced [노아] · <speak='닿아'> pronounced [다아]

Before consonants, it causes aspiration as described above.

Note: ㅎ-batchim is one of the most irregular — memorizing specific cases is the best strategy!

ㄲ.base:
**쌍기역** · tense ㄱ (fortis)

The tense consonants (경음) are unique to Korean — no equivalent in English or most European languages! The throat tightens (glottal tension), no air release at all — <speak='까'>, <speak='꼬'>, <speak='끼'>.

**How to produce:** Try saying ㄱ but "hold back" the air before releasing. The voice feels compressed. Some describe it as the sound of a surprised "ack!".

**ㄱ trio:** ㄱ (plain) · ㅋ (aspirated) · **ㄲ** (tense, no breath)

Common words:
- <speak='까다'> (to peel)
- <speak='끝'> (end)
- <speak='꿈'> (dream)
ㄲ.batchim: As batchim, same as ㄱ — unreleased velar stop.

ㄸ.base:
**쌍디귿** · tense ㄷ (fortis)

Tense version of ㄷ — <speak='따'>, <speak='또'>, <speak='띠'>. Throat contracted, no air release. Feels like a forceful 'tt'.

**ㄷ trio:** ㄷ (plain) · ㅌ (aspirated) · **ㄸ** (tense)

Common words:
- <speak='따뜻하다'> (warm, cozy)
- <speak='또'> (also, again)
- <speak='땅'> (ground, earth)

ㅃ.base:
**쌍비읍** · tense ㅂ (fortis)

Tense version of ㅂ — <speak='빠'>, <speak='뽀'>, <speak='삐'>. Lips pressed with tension, no breath.

**ㅂ trio:** ㅂ (plain) · ㅍ (aspirated) · **ㅃ** (tense)

Common words:
- <speak='빠르다'> (to be fast)
- <speak='빵'> (bread)
- <speak='뽀뽀'> (little kiss)

ㅆ.base:
**쌍시옷** · tense ㅅ (fortis)

Tense version of ㅅ — <speak='싸'>, <speak='쏘'>, <speak='씌'>. Forceful 'ss' with no breath.

**Very common!** Appears in two of the most-used Korean words:
- <speak='있다'> (to exist, to have) → sounds [이따] in fast speech
- <speak='없다'> (to not exist, to not have) → sounds [업따] in fast speech

Other words: <speak='씩'> (each), <speak='쓰다'> (to use / write), <speak='쌀'> (uncooked rice)
ㅆ.batchim: As batchim, same as ㅅ (ㄷ group).

ㅉ.base:
**쌍지읒** · tense ㅈ (fortis)

Tense version of ㅈ — <speak='짜'>, <speak='쪄'>, <speak='쪼'>. Forceful 'jj' with no breath.

**ㅈ trio:** ㅈ (plain) · ㅊ (aspirated) · **ㅉ** (tense)

Common words:
- <speak='짜다'> (to be salty / to squeeze)
- <speak='찌다'> (to gain weight / to steam)
- <speak='짜증'> (irritation, annoyance)

ㅏ.base:
**아** · open vowel 'a'

The simplest, clearest vowel — <speak='아'>. Same as 'a' in "father". Wide open mouth, tongue low. Easy!

Common words:
- <speak='아'> (ah! / older brother — for women)
- <speak='아버지'> (father)
- <speak='사랑'> (love)
- <speak='가다'> (to go)

In Hangul, ㅏ is written to the RIGHT of the initial consonant.

ㅑ.base:
**야** · 'ya' — glide + ㅏ

Starts with a quick 'y' before 'a' — <speak='야'>. Like saying "ya" quickly. Phonetically: semivowel (y) + ㅏ.

Common words:
- <speak='야'> (hey! / field)
- <speak='야구'> (baseball)
- <speak='야채'> (vegetables)
- <speak='야간'> (nighttime)

ㅓ.base:
**어** · vowel 'eo' — the hardest for learners!

ㅓ has no direct English equivalent. It's a **back unrounded vowel** — <speak='어'>.

**How to produce:**
1. Position your tongue as if to say 'uh' (like in "fun")
2. BUT your lips are NOT rounded — neutral or slightly open
3. The sound resonates in the back of the mouth

Compare: <speak='오'> (ㅗ — rounded lips) vs. <speak='어'> (ㅓ — no rounding)

Common words:
- <speak='어'> (hmm, yes — informal response)
- <speak='어머니'> (mother)
- <speak='저'> (I — humble form)
- <speak='뭐'> (what)

ㅕ.base:
**여** · 'yeo' — glide + ㅓ

Starts with quick 'y' before ㅓ — <speak='여'>. One of the most characteristic Korean sounds!

**How to produce:** Quick 'y' + ㅓ (back, unrounded). Hard to mimic without practice.

Common words:
- <speak='여자'> (woman)
- <speak='여기'> (here)
- <speak='여행'> (travel)
- <speak='이야기'> (story, conversation)

ㅗ.base:
**오** · close rounded vowel 'o'

Rounded 'o' with lips in a small circle — <speak='오'>. Similar to 'o' in "go" or "boat". Round lips into a small circle.

Common words:
- <speak='오'> (five / oh!)
- <speak='오다'> (to come)
- <speak='오늘'> (today)
- <speak='소'> (cow)
- <speak='코'> (nose)

In Hangul, ㅗ is written BELOW the initial consonant.

ㅛ.base:
**요** · 'yo' — glide + ㅗ

Quick 'y' before rounded ㅗ — <speak='요'>. Round lips from the start.

Common words:
- <speak='요'> (politeness particle / this one here)
- <speak='요즘'> (these days)
- <speak='요리'> (cooking)
- <speak='요일'> (day of the week)

ㅜ.base:
**우** · close rounded vowel 'u'

'u' with lips projected forward — <speak='우'>. Similar to 'oo' in "food". Lips pushed forward.

Common words:
- <speak='우리'> (we / our)
- <speak='우유'> (milk)
- <speak='물'> (water)
- <speak='구름'> (cloud)
- <speak='두'> (two)

In Hangul, ㅜ is written BELOW the initial consonant (like ㅗ but pointing down).

ㅠ.base:
**유** · 'yu' — glide + ㅜ

Quick 'y' before rounded ㅜ — <speak='유'>. Like 'yu' in "yukon"!

Common words:
- <speak='유명하다'> (to be famous)
- <speak='유럽'> (Europe)
- <speak='이유'> (reason)

ㅡ.base:
**으** · central unrounded vowel 'eu' — no equivalent in English!

ㅡ is probably the hardest vowel for English speakers. It's a **high central unrounded vowel** — <speak='으'>.

**How to produce:**
1. Position your tongue as for 'u' (high and back)
2. BUT do NOT round your lips — keep them spread or neutral
3. Sound comes out without lip projection

Like the "hmm" of hesitation, or the 'uh' in "just".

Compare: <speak='우'> (ㅜ — rounded) vs. <speak='으'> (ㅡ — unrounded)

Very common in Korean! Appears in almost every verb in basic form:
- <speak='가다'> (go) · <speak='먹다'> (eat) · <speak='크다'> (be big)

ㅣ.base:
**이** · high front vowel 'i'

The simplest vowel — <speak='이'>. Same as 'ee' in "see". Easy!

One of the Korean nominative particles is 이/가 — one of the first things you learn in grammar!

Common words:
- <speak='이'> (this / nominative particle / two)
- <speak='이름'> (name)
- <speak='이상하다'> (to be strange)
- <speak='기쁘다'> (to be happy)

In Hangul, ㅣ is written to the RIGHT of the initial consonant.

ㅐ.base:
**애** · vowel 'ae'

Historically distinct from ㅔ (like 'ae' in Latin), but in **modern Korean** ㅐ and ㅔ are pronounced **identically** — <speak='애'>.

Don't worry about distinguishing them in speech! Even native young Koreans no longer distinguish them. The difference exists only in writing.

Common words:
- <speak='애'> (kid / face)
- <speak='애기'> (baby — affectionate)
- <speak='사랑해'> (I love you!)
- <speak='왜냐하면'> (because)

ㅒ.base:
**얘** · 'yae' — glide + ㅐ

Quick 'y' before ㅐ — <speak='얘'>. Since ㅐ = ㅔ in modern speech, ㅒ and ㅖ sound identical.

Relatively rare. Most common in:
- <speak='얘기'> (story, chat — shortened from 이야기)
- <speak='얘'> (this person — informal reference)

ㅔ.base:
**에** · vowel 'e'

Sounds like 'e' in "red" — <speak='에'>. Identical to ㅐ in modern Korean. Only the spelling differs.

Common words:
- <speak='에'> (location / direction particle)
- <speak='에서'> (at, from — location particle)
- <speak='세'> (three)
- <speak='게임'> (game)
- <speak='케이크'> (cake)

ㅖ.base:
**예** · 'ye' — glide + ㅔ

Quick 'y' before ㅔ — <speak='예'>. Very common!

Common words:
- <speak='예'> (yes — formal)
- <speak='예쁘다'> (to be pretty)
- <speak='예약'> (reservation)
- <speak='예습'> (pre-study)
- <speak='예의'> (manners, etiquette)

ㅘ.base:
**와** · 'wa' — ㅗ + ㅏ fused

ㅗ and ㅏ combined into one gliding vowel — <speak='와'>. Like saying "wa" quickly.

Common words:
- <speak='와'> (wow! / and — particle)
- <speak='와요'> (come! / to come — polite)
- <speak='봐요'> (look! / to see — polite)
- <speak='와인'> (wine)
- <speak='과일'> (fruit)

ㅙ.base:
**왜** · 'wae' — ㅗ + ㅐ fused

Pronounced as <speak='왜'>. In modern Korean, sounds **identical** to ㅚ and ㅞ — all merged into a single "we" sound.

Most important word with this vowel:
- <speak='왜'> (why?)

Also in: <speak='왜냐하면'> (because)

ㅚ.base:
**외** · 'oe'

Historically like German/Swedish 'ö' (front rounded vowel), but in modern Korean sounds **identical** to ㅙ and ㅞ — <speak='외'>.

Common words:
- <speak='외국'> (foreign country)
- <speak='외국어'> (foreign language)
- <speak='외래어'> (loanword)
- <speak='최고'> (the best)

ㅝ.base:
**워** · 'wo/weo' — ㅜ + ㅓ fused

Combination of ㅜ and ㅓ — <speak='워'>. Lips start rounded (ㅜ) and glide to unrounded ㅓ. Sound between "wo" and "wuh".

Common words:
- <speak='뭐'> (what? — colloquial)
- <speak='봐요'> (look, see — polite)
- <speak='워크숍'> (workshop)

ㅞ.base:
**웨** · 'we' — ㅜ + ㅔ fused

Pronounced as <speak='웨'>. In modern Korean, identical to ㅙ and ㅚ. Very rare in native words — mainly appears in loanwords.

Words:
- <speak='웨이터'> (waiter — loanword)
- <speak='웨딩'> (wedding — loanword)

ㅟ.base:
**위** · 'wi' — ㅜ + ㅣ fused

Historically 'ü' (like French "lune" or German "über"), but most modern Koreans pronounce it as 'wi' — <speak='위'>.

Common words:
- <speak='위'> (above / stomach)
- <speak='위험하다'> (to be dangerous)
- <speak='귀'> (ear)
- <speak='쉬다'> (to rest)

ㅢ.base:
**의** · the most complex vowel in Korean!

ㅢ is a combination of ㅡ + ㅣ and has **three different pronunciations** depending on context:

**1. At word start:** sounds like 'ui' — <speak='의사'> (doctor), <speak='의자'> (chair)

**2. In non-initial syllables:** simplifies to 'i' — <speak='회의'> sounds [회이] · <speak='주의'> sounds [주이]

**3. As the possessive particle 의 (of, 's):** sounds like 'e' — <speak='나의'> sounds [나에] (my)

**Summary:** 의사[의사] · 회의[회이] · 나의[나에]

This variation is completely normal and expected in modern Korean!
